description: Common pitfalls, undefined behavior, and language-specific gotchas.

facts.context: Undefined behavior in C/C++ allows compilers to:
facts.truth: assume it never happens, optimizing aggressively
distractors: generate safe fallback code handling all cases
emit warnings but produce correct code anyway
crash immediately when undefined behavior occurs

facts.context: Signed integer overflow in C is undefined because:
facts.truth: it enables optimizations assuming no overflow
distractors: hardware always traps on signed overflow conditions
unsigned overflow is also undefined behavior
the C standard forgot to define the behavior

facts.context: JavaScript == vs === differ in that === is:
facts.truth: strict equality with no type coercion
distractors: looser equality allowing more matches
deprecated and should never be used
identical to == in all circumstances

facts.context: Mutable default arguments in Python (def f(x=[])) cause bugs because:
facts.truth: defaults are shared across all calls
distractors: a new list is created for every function call
Python prohibits mutable default arguments
the default is copied before each call
floating point arithmetic is completely random
IEEE 754 defines equality as approximate only
the CPU hardware has bugs in its FPU

facts.context: Null pointer dereference in C causes undefined behavior, not guaranteed crash, because:
facts.truth: some systems map address zero to valid memory
distractors: null pointers always point to valid memory
the operating system always catches null access
C guarantees segmentation fault on null access
iterators are used after container destruction only
two iterators point to the same element
iterators are copied to another variable

facts.context: JavaScript closure loop capture bug (var in for loop) occurs because:
facts.truth: var has function scope, not block scope
distractors: closures cannot capture loop variables ever
JavaScript prohibits closures inside loops
let and var behave identically in loops

facts.context: String comparison with == vs equals() in Java differs because:
distractors: equals() is deprecated in favor of == always
both compare string contents identically
== is faster and should always be preferred

facts.context: Off-by-one errors in array indexing are common because:
distractors: all programming languages use one-based indexing
arrays automatically extend when accessed out of bounds
compilers always catch array bounds errors
